bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the appointment as an emotional, family-centered transition, stressing the “scramble,” “family affair,” and Nordone’s lack of elected-office experience, while right-leaning outlets present it as a strategic, practical move to preserve a “fragile majority” and “restore” GOP control before filing deadlines.

The left also highlights Trump’s role as political coordination, whereas the right spotlights his recommendation and praises Nordone as a “wonderful sister” or “done deal.” The key dividing lines are experience versus continuity, and sentiment versus Senate math.
Both sides still agree on the sudden death, interim nature, and special election backdrop.
summary: Governor Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham Nordone to fill the U.S. Senate seat vacated by her brother, Lindsey Graham, serving until the term expires on Jan. 3, 2027.
Lindsey Graham died Saturday night after an aortic dissection, prompting the vacancy that Trump suggested McMaster fill by selecting Graham's sister via a Truth Social post.
A special primary must be held by Aug. 11, with candidates able to file beginning July 21 through July 28, and a runoff scheduled for Aug. 25 if no candidate secures 50% of the vote.
Greenville County Republican Party Chairman Jeff Davis said, "I'm expecting a lot of new names to come in," as candidates prepare to leverage existing infrastructure for the race.
Whoever wins the special Republican primary will face Democratic nominee Annie Andrews in November, setting up a competitive general election for Graham's seat.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the lawsuit as a protective antitrust move, stressing “block,” “suing,” and the $110 billion scale as a public-interest check on consolidation, often adding worker and theater concerns; right-leaning coverage turns the same fight into a partisan clash, using combative terms like “war,” “regulatory goons,” and “corporate exodus” to portray California as hostile and to spotlight fears the company may “ditch” the state.
Center coverage stays more procedural, focusing on the filing and legal stakes.
Both sides agree on the multistate suit and federal approval, but diverge sharply on whether this is consumer protection or blue-state overreach.
summary: On Monday, 12 states, including California, filed an antitrust lawsuit to block the $111 billion takeover of Warner Bros. Discovery by Paramount Skydance Corporation.
The Department of Justice approved the takeover in June, but state attorneys are challenging the deal, mirroring legal actions against Live Nation Entertainment and prior Nexstar-Tegna litigation.
Bonta stated the merger would lead to higher prices, lower quality, and less content for consumers. He added, "In this country, no one is above the law."
Paramount said in a statement to CNN that antitrust authorities globally reviewed the transaction and concluded it does not violate competition laws.
The United Kingdom government is poised to pursue antitrust action against the Paramount-Warner deal, signaling potential international regulatory challenges ahead.
title: California, 11 states suing to block Paramount's $110 billion Warner Bros deal

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s move as a dangerous escalation, using loaded terms like “extortion racket,” “pirate,” and “massive escalation” to stress illegality, war risk, and broader chaos, while right-leaning coverage portrays it as tough protection—“guardian,” “fairness,” and “reimposes” recast the blockade and 20% fee as reimbursement for securing a vital route.
The key fault line is whether the strait is being seized or safeguarded, with legal basis and implementation details de-emphasized on the left but foregrounded on the right and center.
All sides agree on the blockade, fee, and oil-market shock, reflecting deeper divides over power, sovereignty, and deterrence.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump announced the U.S. is reinstating a naval blockade on Iranian vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, declaring America "THE GUARDIAN OF THE HORMUZ STRAIT" and imposing a 20% security fee on all transiting cargo.

The collapse of the June memorandum of understanding shattered the interim peace agreement, as renewed exchanges of fire between U.S. forces and Iran prompted this policy shift following failed negotiations.

U.S. Central Command struck roughly 140 targets across Iran on Sunday, including missile and drone launch sites, degrading Iranian naval capabilities after the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps launched retaliatory attacks.
Vessel traffic through the waterway declined by about 52% over July 10-12, while Brent crude oil prices jumped nearly 5% on Monday, reflecting immediate market disruption and shipping concerns.

The International Maritime Organization warned there is "no legal basis" for mandatory transit tolls, creating enforcement hurdles as the U.S. seeks to implement the "Guardian" security model amid regional instability.
title: Trump says US will reinstate Strait of Hormuz blockade and impose 20% charge on cargo

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Biddeford shooting as another instance of ICE violence, using charged phrases like “killed another person,” “brutality,” and “anti-immigrant,” and stressing calls for body-camera evidence and accountability; right-leaning coverage instead centers the alleged threat, leaning on terms like “self-defense,” “mow down,” and “tried to run over” to portray agents as responding to danger.
The pivotal dividing line is whether the driver’s actions justify force, with the left treating that claim skeptically and the right treating it as the story’s core.
Center coverage stays procedural and cautious.
All sides agree on a fatal ICE-involved shooting, but diverge over enforcement, immigration , and state power.
summary: On Monday, July 13, 2026, U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents fatally shot a person in Biddeford, Maine, prompting investigations by state police and the FBI.

The shooting occurred amid the Trump administration's 'Operation Catch of the Day' enforcement surge in Maine, which detained nearly 200 people but faced scrutiny after only 11 had criminal convictions.

Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told Senator Angus King the agent fired after the motorist "weaponized" his vehicle, yet witnesses disputed this account and noted agents lacked body cameras.

Representative Chellie Pingree demanded answers about ICE's presence in Maine, while advocacy groups organized protests and called for accountability following the death of the 26-year-old Colombian man.

The killing marks at least the 11th fatal shooting involving ICE or Border Patrol agents since President Donald Trump took office, intensifying scrutiny of the administration's mass deportation campaign.
title: 1 person killed in Maine shooting involving ICE, state House speaker says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the strikes as escalation and diplomatic damage, using terms like “unleashes,” “standoff,” and “degrading” to stress regional instability and the way Trump’s action can “reduce to nothing” peace efforts.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, casts the same operation as forceful protection of “shipping” and “freedom of navigation,” leaning on words like “accountable,” “taking over,” and even Trump’s boastful “bombed the hell out of them” to signal resolve.
The key divide is whether the strikes are a risky provocation or justified security action, though both sides agree the conflict is widening around the Strait of Hormuz.
summary: U.S. Central Command launched new strikes against Iran on Sunday to degrade its ability to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, following a massive Saturday operation that hit about 140 Iranian military targets.

Iranian missile and drone assaults on American allies across the Middle East , including Kuwait, Jordan, and Qatar, prompted the escalation after Iran targeted a Cyprus-flagged merchant vessel in the Strait.

Conflicting declarations emerged Sunday regarding the Strait of Hormuz's status, as CENTCOM asserted, "FACT: Iran does not control the Strait of Hormuz," while Tehran claimed the waterway was closed until further notice.
President Donald Trump stated Sunday on NBC's Meet the Press, "We bombed the hell out of them last night," while U.S. officials warned to expect a "bigger" wave of attacks.
These latest strikes raise concerns about whether the broader fighting can be contained, as the White House seeks to pursue talks despite declaring the June ceasefire agreement over last week.
title: The US launches more strikes on Iran as the standoff over the Strait of Hormuz escalates

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the story as an escalating bombing campaign—using phrases like “fresh salvo” and “deal... falters”—to stress widening war and the high cost on Tehran, while right-leaning coverage turns the same strikes into a defensive, security-first response, leaning on words like “BREAKING,” “launches,” “powerful,” and “degrade” to portray decisive action against threats to civilians and commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
The biggest split is whether the strikes are seen as punitive escalation or protection, with right coverage also adding Iranian retaliation, U.S. ships, and bases.
Both sides agree the conflict is intensifying and that Trump’s direction matters.
summary: On Monday, US Central Command launched a third consecutive night of strikes against Iranian forces as President Donald Trump announced reinstatement of a naval blockade in the Strait of Hormuz.
Escalating missile and drone exchanges over the past week cast doubt on an interim US-Iranian agreement signed last month to halt hostilities and pursue 60 days of negotiations.
Trump called for a 20% reimbursement on all cargo shipped through the Strait, while MarineTraffic reported vessel activity declined by about 52% over July 10-12 compared to the previous week.

Warning that continued military interference could trigger greater incidents, the Revolutionary Guards and Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baghaei claimed US pressure on Oman hindered shipping management efforts.
title: U.S. Launches New Iran Strikes, as Trump Weighs Attacking Nuclear Site

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the move as a harsh “ban” or “block,” stressing government overreach and the burden on citizens—especially with words like “temporary,” “own citizens,” and the 21-day wait in a third country—while also spotlighting the administration’s role and the HHS order.

Right-Leaning coverage pivots to public-health urgency, using sharper warning language like “do not travel ,” “deadly communicable disease,” and “serious risk,” plus outbreak stats to underscore danger.
Center coverage stays more procedural, emphasizing delayed repatriation.
Both sides agree Ebola drives the policy, but they diverge on whether the story is about restriction or risk.
summary: The Trump administration blocked American citizens in the Democratic Republic of Congo from traveling to the U.S. on commercial flights on Monday, citing a widening Ebola outbreak that has spread to multiple provinces.
Health and Human Services Secretary Robert Kennedy signed the order after the virus spread to areas near Kinshasa, with confirmed cases rising to 1,926 and 702 deaths by Sunday, official data showed.
Under Title 49, the order places affected Americans on a "do-not-board" list, requiring them to spend at least 21 days in a third country before returning to the U.S.

Some two dozen Americans set to board flights on Tuesday face travel delays, while the State Department provides support during the waiting period and the CDC monitors a citizen who tested positive for Bundibugyo Ebola.

The often-fatal disease spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids from infected people or animals, causing high fever, vomiting, and internal and external bleeding, underscoring why officials maintain strict travel restrictions as the outbreak expands.
title: US to block citizens in Congo from immediate travel home: Reuters

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s moves as dangerous escalation—“war,” “third night,” “blockade,” and “destruction” signal a widening Middle East conflict, with Iran retaliation and Strait of Hormuz fallout pushed to the fore.

Right-Leaning coverage is more hawkish and combative, using terms like “unleashes,” “attacks,” “take out,” and “all-out war” to portray strikes as forceful pressure, while spotlighting whether Pickaxe Mountain is a fortified nuclear site beyond bunker-buster reach.
Center coverage stays more restrained, balancing strikes with Trump’s claim a deal is “still possible.” Both sides agree on the facts, but split over whether they imply menace or leverage.
summary: On Tuesday, the United Arab Emirates reported Iran struck two tankers in the Strait of Hormuz with missiles, killing one crew member and wounding eight others, as CENTCOM conducted a third consecutive night of strikes on Iranian military targets.

Hostilities resumed last week after the interim U.S.-Iran agreement collapsed, prompting President Donald Trump to reimpose a blockade on Iranian ports and threaten a 20% toll on cargo transiting the waterway.

Oil prices surged more than 9% as confirmed crossings through the strait declined by around 52%; CENTCOM successfully struck military targets including Bushehr and Bandar Abbas, while Iran retaliated against U.S. interests in Bahrain.

Despite escalating attacks, President Trump said on Monday a deal to end the war remains 'still possible,' though Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei stated the June memorandum of understanding is 'in crisis.'

The administration notified Congress that hostilities resumed on July 7, opening a 60-day window for military operations without congressional approval, while the UAE Defense Ministry stated it remains on 'highest level of readiness' against threats.
title: Trump Signals Possible Strike on Iran's Pickaxe Mountain Nuclear Facility

summary: Senator Jim Banks plans to introduce the Citizenship Act to end birthright citizenship for children of unauthorized immigrants and birth tourists, labeling their parents as 'invaders' under federal law following a recent Supreme Court ruling.

The bill amends the Immigration and Nationality Act to exclude children of unauthorized entrants, citing legal precedents such as U.S. v. Wong Kim Ark and codifying language from Trump's 2025 executive order.

Justice Brett Kavanaugh's concurrence in the Supreme Court's Trump v. Barbara decision supports Congress's authority to amend federal statutes to define exceptions to birthright citizenship, although the Court upheld birthright citizenship under the 14th Amendment.

The Citizenship Act avoids amending the Constitution directly and aims to prevent exploitation of the immigration system, which is expected to prompt legal challenges over the definition of citizenship.
title: Sen. Jim Banks Introduces Bill to Restrict Birthright Citizenship for Children of Unauthorized Immigrants

bias: Left-Leaning coverage treats the White House IndyCar event as a light spectacle, emphasizing the custom helmet and a “giddy” Trump, while right-leaning coverage turns it into a patriotic showcase, using phrases like “Freedom 250,” “racing legends,” and “like no other race ever” to frame it as triumphant and historic.
The biggest split is over meaning: left de-emphasizes the route and grandeur, right highlights Pennsylvania Avenue, the National Mall, and America’s semiquincentennial.
Center coverage sits between them, stressing the first-ever capital race and Trump’s executive-order role.
Both sides agree on the basic event, but not on its significance.
summary: On Monday, July 13, 2026, President Donald Trump hosts IndyCar officials and drivers at the White House for a "Grand Prix Showcase," previewing the upcoming Freedom 250 Grand Prix street race in Washington.
Trump set the race in motion with a January 2026 executive order, which directed the White House Task Force on Celebrating America's 250th Birthday to designate a route through the capital.
Scheduled for August 22-23, the street circuit spans 1.7 miles near the National Mall, with cars reaching 190 mph along Pennsylvania Avenue and Independence Avenue.
IndyCar owner Roger Penske praised the initiative, saying, "President Trump has bestowed an incredible distinction upon our sport," while noting the event celebrates the country's independence.
Beyond the race, the Freedom 250 initiative includes the 2026 Patriot Games, an athletic competition featuring challenges designed to test strength, speed, leadership, and resilience.
title: Trump hosts top IndyCar drivers at White House ahead of first-ever capital race

summary: On Monday, July 13, 2026, an anonymous protest group called The Secret Handshake installed a 10-foot-tall 'Iran War Participation Trophy' on the National Mall to satirize President Donald Trump 's involvement in the ongoing conflict.
Mocking the President's 'enthusiastic involvement' in the Iran War, the installation comes as the conflict remains unpopular with the American public, despite bipartisan Congressional rebukes.

Known for previous satirical displays like a gold toilet, the Secret Handshake invited tourists to leave personal trophies at the monument's base, suggesting Trump might claim credit for those awards as well.

White House spokesperson Anna Kelly criticized the installation, stating 'artists' should 'refrain from weighing in on foreign policy' and claiming the United States Military achieved objectives during 'Operation Epic Fury.'
The statue will remain on the National Mall for several days, while the group hopes public donations will lead the President to 'be perceived as a winner' rather than bombing other countries.
title: New Statue on the National Mall Criticizes Trump’s Handling of Iran Conflict
Denton County reports first human West Nile case of 2026

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the center frames Chen’s detention as an “irritant” in a “tense relationship,” stressing diplomatic management and even Trump’s need to keep ties steady.

Right-Leaning coverage, by contrast, foregrounds confrontation: words like “reportedly,” “charges,” “ espionage ,” and “significant implications” cast China as an adversarial power and elevate the case into a broader U.S.-China security clash.
The dividing line is whether the story is chiefly a diplomatic nuisance or a warning sign of hostile state behavior.
Both sides still converge on the core facts—detention, North Korea-linked research, and prolonged confinement—reflecting shared concern over escalating geopolitical strain.
summary: On Monday, July 13, 2026, Chinese-born American seismologist Youlin Chen faced an espionage trial in China after nearly two years of detention, according to his wife, Yufang Rong, and advocacy groups.
Chinese state security officers arrested Chen at Beijing International Airport on November 5, 2024, as he prepared to return to Boston after visiting family and lecturing at two universities.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio designated Chen as "wrongfully detained" on March 19, 2026, while Rong reported Chen has lost 30 to 40 pounds and endured "harsh conditions" including forced immobility and inadequate medical care.

Rong fears Beijing pre-determined a guilty verdict, which carries a possible life sentence or death penalty in espionage cases, though President Donald Trump raised the detention with Chinese leader Xi Jinping during a May 2026 state visit.

U.S. Senator Ed Markey remains "deeply concerned about Dr. Chen's safety and wellbeing," urging the Chinese government to release him as the case looms ahead of a planned September visit by Chinese leader Xi Jinping.
title: China detains US seismologist who has studied North Korean nuclear tests

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Bastille Day parade as Macron’s “last” showpiece: a “massive demonstration weapon” tied to “rearmament,” “strategic autonomy,” and a broader “European awakening,” with Ukraine support cast as a strategic response to U.S. disengagement and eastern-front threats.

Right-Leaning coverage is more ceremonial, portraying it as a “massive” national holiday event that will “honor Ukraine and its allies,” while de-emphasizing the wider geopolitical argument; its sharpest pivot is the mention of Ukrainian troops and officials in Paris for talks.
Both sides converge on Europe ’s support for Ukraine and the parade’s scale, but they diverge on whether the spectacle signals strategic resolve or chiefly diplomatic pageantry.
summary: President Emmanuel Macron hosted a Bastille Day parade in Paris with about 30 other leaders to show European unity and military support for Ukraine.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy attended, and Ukrainian troops and co-pilots trained in France participated alongside European forces in the parade.
The parade included 6,800 troops, setting a new record compared to the previous year, with participation from multiple European countries' aircraft.
Due to forest fires and a heat wave, traditional fireworks and firefighters' balls were canceled in some regions, including Paris, but the Eiffel Tower drone light show continued.
title: Macron Hosts Record Bastille Day Parade in Paris for Ukraine

bias: Left and center coverage frames Trump’s support for the bipartisan Russia sanctions bill as momentum-building and a “fitting tribute” to Graham, stressing procedural movement, “hopeful” Senate passage, and “smoothing the path,” while the right leans harder into legacy politics , calling it one of Graham’s “most important achievements” and pushing “immediate” action.
The rhetoric differs too: the left/center use subdued, policy-driven language, whereas the right adds sharper cues like “long-stalled” and “ramp up pressure,” signaling urgency.
The key dividing line is whether Graham’s death is a legislative catalyst or a commemorative trigger, though all sides agree on Trump’s support, bipartisan backing, and pressure on Russia.
summary: On Monday, a White House official confirmed President Donald Trump supports the bipartisan Russia sanctions package spearheaded by the late Senator Lindsey Graham, potentially clearing the legislative path forward.

Senator Lindsey Graham's unexpected death on July 11 reshaped the legislative effort he championed since April 2025, which originally proposed secondary tariffs as high as 500 per cent on Russian energy buyers.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune is assessing the package, which proponents believe could secure 80 to 90 votes, while South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster appointed Darline Graham Nordone on Monday to finish Graham's term.
Despite administration support, Trump sounded less committed when asked Monday evening, saying "We'll decide very soon on that," while Senator Rand Paul vowed to fight fast-tracking the bill.

The sanctions package aims to weaken Moscow amid its war with Ukraine, though the official legislative text remains unreleased, leaving senators to debate final language while competing priorities consume Senate floor time.
title: White House says Trump supports Graham’s Russia sanctions bill

summary: On Monday, suspended Montana Public Service Commissioner Brad Molnar asked a state district court judge to block Governor Greg Gianforte from appointing his replacement, seeking a preliminary injunction until the court rules on his request to restore his office.

Governor Greg Gianforte suspended Molnar without pay for one year last Thursday after the Public Service Commission requested his removal following investigations into allegations of sexual harassment, discrimination, and retaliatory behavior toward agency employees.
Molnar argued he received "government by accusation" rather than proper due process, claiming he was denied robust cross-examination rights that employees facing termination normally possess.

The court filing argues that blocking an "unlawfully seated appointee" prevents immediate harm, as a replacement would cast votes on the pending $3.6 billion NorthWestern Energy-Black Hills Energy merger of significant consequence to Montana ratepayers.

Gianforte described the suspension as "unprecedented" but warranted by Molnar's conduct, though his office noted Monday the governor is still evaluating next steps for appointing a Public Service Commission replacement.
title: Montana Governor Suspends PSC Commissioner Brad Molnar

summary: Wyoming state representative Lloyd Larsen and the nonprofit group Wyoming Friends for Martin's Cove proposed a land exchange to transfer 933 Bureau of Land Management acres surrounding Martin's Cove to the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.

Martin's Cove holds historical significance as the 1856 refuge site for a stranded handcart company; the church currently holds a 25-year lease from the Bureau of Land Management, though previous management efforts sparked controversy and litigation.

Parcels identified for the exchange include a 358-acre tract near Independence Rock and 575 acres between Miller Springs and Savage Peak, which proponents argue secures wildlife habitat and preserves historic sites.

Support from three former Wyoming governors—Matt Mead, Dave Freudenthal, and Mike Sullivan—and former Interior Department appointee Rob Wallace strengthens the effort, with the Bureau of Land Management stating it will follow Congressional direction.

Julia Stuble, Wyoming director for The Wilderness Society, praised the proponents' "sound process" for prioritizing public interest, while advocates draft legislation aiming for congressional introduction this summer.
title: LDS church would get sacred site, Martin’s Cove, under proposed BLM land trade now gaining steam

bias: Left-Leaning coverage leans into the scam’s mechanics and menace, using terms like “dangerous,” “completely scammed,” and “remarkably sophisticated” to stress how convincingly the caller mimicked a bank fraud team with badge ID and personal details; right-leaning coverage keeps the drama but broadens it, framing the incident as “nearly loses life savings” and hinting at a growing scam trend, with another victim used as evidence.

Center-Leaning coverage is more restrained, calling it “alarming” and a “very scary experience.” All three agree on near-loss and deception, but they diverge on whether the story is an isolated scare or a wider fraud warning.
summary: Recently, CBS News correspondent Matt Gutman revealed he was the target of a sophisticated bank impersonation scam, where a caller nearly convinced him to withdraw all his funds by claiming to investigate fraud.
Posing as fraud protection, the scammer provided a name and badge ID before instructing Gutman to withdraw cash, warning him not to speak to tellers because alleged fraudsters worked at his branch.

Gutman admitted he spent over an hour on the phone before realizing the absurdity of using a civilian for a sting operation, noting the scheme appeared legitimate because scammers knew personal details.
While Gutman did not lose money, he lost time during the ordeal, describing it as a 'very scary experience' and expressing gratitude for support from authorities and his bank.
Warning that these scams are incredibly sophisticated, Gutman advised that if contacted about account issues, people should verify requests by calling the number on the back of their bank card.
title: CBS News Anchor Says Los Angeles Scam Nearly Wiped Out Life Savings

bias: Left and center coverage frames the story as a solemn casualty update, stressing the “official tally” and the “death toll” rising to 14, while the right sharpens it into a broader war-and-burden narrative with “US Iran war ,” “wounded,” and “traumatic brain injuries,” making the scale feel larger and more alarming.
The key divide is emphasis: left-leaning language foregrounds loss and consequence, right-leaning language underscores military severity and escalation, and center coverage stays more report-like.

Shared ground remains the same core facts—14 deaths, a Navy pilot’s crash, and official military figures—but the framing reflects deeper differences in how conflict, sacrifice, and national-security stakes are interpreted.
summary: The Pentagon's official death toll in the Iran war has risen to 14 service members following a Navy pilot's helicopter crash in the Arabian Sea in early July.
This death marks the first recorded fatality since 13 service members died in separate March incidents, including an Iranian drone strike in Kuwait and a KC-135 crash in Iraq.
The number of wounded troops has grown to 414 as of Monday, with an Air Force member added to the casualty list this week.
Central Command spokeswoman Emma Thompson said "almost all" injured personnel have returned to duty, though the majority suffered traumatic brain injuries from explosions.
While Iran and the military have resumed strikes, Central Command offered no details on the specific Air Force member, leaving unclear whether recent action caused the injury.
title: US military death toll in Iran war rises to 14 after Navy pilot death this month

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the Cuba trip as a humanitarian critique, stressing that Democratic lawmakers “compare” the embargo to “silent Gaza” and leaning into the suffering implied by that phrase; center coverage stays closer to wire-style description, adding names and dateline context without much extra spin; right-leaning coverage is tighter and more skeptical in effect, stripping away context and spotlighting the provocative comparison itself.
The key dividing line is whether “silent Gaza” is treated as a moral indictment of U.S. policy or as a loaded political claim by Democrats.
Both sides agree on the trip and Trump’s embargo, but their emphasis reflects broader ideological splits over humanitarian framing versus partisan scrutiny.
summary: Four Democratic members of the U.S. Congress visited Cuba and described the U.S. energy embargo as turning the island into a 'silent Gaza'.
The U.S. imposed the energy embargo in January after the capture of Venezuela's then-President Nicolás Maduro and threatened tariffs on countries selling fuel to Cuba.
There are no current talks between Washington and Havana about lifting the energy embargo.
Congress member Pocan said that Marco Rubio is making the embargo issue personal, which he disagrees with.
title: Democratic members of US Congress visit Cuba and compare US energy embargo to ‘silent Gaza’

bias: Left-Leaning coverage turns the cash shortfall into a story of campaign mismanagement, using sharper terms like “finance disaster,” “mismanagement,” and the firing of the manager to show internal fallout while noting Rodriguez is staying in the race; right-leaning coverage mostly de-emphasized that drama and stays with the leaner “far less money than she thought” framing, treating it as a reported fundraising problem.

The center adds the most overt accountability angle, stressing she “failed to take responsibility” amid “serious, mounting questions.” All sides agree on unpaid invoices and hundreds of thousands less cash, but they diverge on whether the episode is a temporary stumble or a broader competence failure.
summary: Wisconsin Democratic gubernatorial candidate Sara Rodriguez announced Monday that her campaign has far less cash than expected after ads slated to run last week failed to air due to unpaid invoices.

After discovering contributions were double-counted and expenses undercounted, Rodriguez fired her campaign manager late Sunday, resulting in the campaign having far less money than previously thought.
Notifying the Wisconsin Ethics Commission on Monday, Rodriguez said her campaign raised about $1 million and had about $200,000 cash on hand while correcting reports filed last year.
Democratic challenger Joel Brennan called the financial errors "disqualifying," while Rodriguez vowed to remain in the race despite the accounting discrepancies.
Rodriguez's next financial report is due Wednesday, preceding the Aug. 11 primary where the winner will advance to face Republican Rep. Tom Tiffany in the general election.
title: Wisconsin governor candidate says her campaign has far less money than she thought

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the move as Trump “guts” or “decimating” protections, stressing sacred Native American lands, conservation harm, and a “push” that “sparking a new fight”; right-leaning coverage recasts it as “correcting federal overreach,” “putting Utah communities ahead,” and even calls the prior boundaries a “land grab,” with rhetoric like “slashes” and “How Dare” signaling approval and anti-Washington populism.
The dividing line is whether the 90% cut and nearly 3 million acres are portrayed as protection lost or local control regained.
Both sides note the same scale and legal stakes, but they invert the meaning of development access.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump issued an executive order reducing the boundaries of Bears Ears National Monument and Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument in Utah, undoing protections established by previous administrations on sacred public lands.

Trump Interior Secretary Doug Burgum initiated the review to expand U.S. energy production, fulfilling a goal of Utah officials who long argued the state should control its own lands for mining coal and uranium.

Davina Smith-Idjesa, co-chair of the Bears Ears Inter-Tribal Coalition, called the reduction "heartbreaking," saying the area is a "living cultural site" holding tribal histories, ceremonies, and ancestral footprints.

The executive action triggers new legal challenges over whether a president has authority under the 1906 Antiquities Act to alter boundaries of existing monuments, a power legally contested between federal and state officials.

Administration policies aim to tap natural resource wealth across more than 100,000 square miles of federal lands, reversing the Biden-era goal to conserve at least 30% of U.S. lands and waters by 2030.
title: Trump shrinks Bears Ears, Grand Staircase-Escalante national monuments in Utah

bias: Left-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized here, while the center and right split sharply: the right frames the $5.56 billion as a win for an “ongoing” healthcare-fraud “crackdown,” stressing recoveries, “cost savings,” and even a breakdown of the totals; the center counters with skepticism, using phrases like “more complicated picture,” “complicates,” and “unprecedented crackdown” to question whether enforcement is actually accelerating.

The key dividing lines are the enforcement slowdown, the “two-year low,” and the 1,212 exclusions, which the center treats as context for a weaker narrative, while the right treats the same report as proof of successful anti-fraud oversight.
summary: On Monday, the Department of Health and Human Services Office of Inspector General reported $5.56 billion in expected recoveries and projected savings over six months, barring 1,212 individuals and companies from federal programs.

Vice President JD Vance, HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., and Medicare chief Mehmet Oz promote what the White House has called an "unrelenting" campaign against healthcare fraud in Medicaid and Medicare Advantage programs.

Enforcement metrics hit a two-year low, with combined criminal and civil actions falling to 604 and criminal referrals dropping to 1,168 from 1,451, complicating the administration's portrayal of an unprecedented crackdown.

Audits into autism-related Medicaid spending uncovered administrative failures like missing documentation rather than organized criminal schemes, though the administration continues to cite such spending as evidence of rampant fraud.

The report's "total monetary impact" metric, which includes projected savings rather than just recovered funds, has swung from $16.61 billion to $2.43 billion to the current $5.56 billion since 2025, complicating year-to-year comparisons.
title: US health watchdog expects $5.56 billion in recoveries and savings

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds the scientific caveat—“no firm scientific evidence”—to blunt the Tylenol -autism connection, while right-leaning coverage amplifies conflict and consequence, using sharper phrases like “overstepped its gatekeeping function” and “key risk” to frame the revived lawsuits as judicial overreach and a threat to investors amid the $50B Kimberly-Clark deal.
Both sides mention the court reviving “hundreds” of lawsuits and the disputed “linking” language, but the left stresses caution and the right stresses suspicion and market fallout.
The center sits between them, adding procedural detail about excluded expert testimony and remand.
summary: A federal appeals court has reinstated over 500 private lawsuits against Tylenol maker Kenvue, breathing new life into claims that prenatal exposure to acetaminophen causes autism and ADHD.

The 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled the lower court overstepped its gatekeeping role, finding that U.S. District Judge Denise Cote improperly excluded qualified expert testimony on a complex, contested scientific topic.
The ruling sent the cases back to federal court in Manhattan for further review, though the panel made clear it was not deciding whether Tylenol actually causes neurodevelopmental disorders.

Kenvue maintains that extensive scientific research shows Tylenol is safe for pregnant women, supported by medical organizations and the FDA, which does not mandate specific autism or ADHD warnings on the drug.

The legal reversal introduces major financial uncertainty for Kenvue, causing its stock to drop following the news, particularly as the company prepares to be acquired by Kimberly-Clark in a $40 billion deal.
title: US appeals court revives hundreds of private lawsuits linking Tylenol to autism

bias: Left and center coverage frame the strike inside a wider regional escalation, recalling U.S. strikes on Iran and a broader cluster of violence, while the right side strips it down to a more direct, security-first picture of Iran hitting a U.S. base.

The tone stays restrained across all three, but the wording still matters: “targets,” “ballistic missiles,” and the call to “dismantle American bases” carry a sharp anti-U.S. edge, even as all outlets attribute the claim to the IRGC.
The key divide is context versus compression, with the right de-emphasized on broader implications and the left more expansive, yet all agree on the core Tuesday event.
summary: The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps targeted a U.S. air base in Jordan with ballistic missiles and drones, claiming the attack set fire to several fuel and ammunition depots.

Jordan's military intercepted and shot down four of the incoming Iranian missiles, reporting no immediate casualties or damage, and previously disputed Iran's claims of successful strikes on its base.
Alongside the strike, the IRGC issued a statement to the Jordanian public calling for the dismantling of U.S. military bases in the country while assuring them of Iran's goodwill.
The Iranian attack followed a five-hour wave of U.S. airstrikes by Central Command targeting Iranian coastal defenses, missile and drone sites, and maritime capabilities.
This intense U.S. operation marked the third consecutive night of American military strikes against Iranian targets ordered by President Donald Trump.
title: Iran says it struck US air base in Jordan, US military ends five hours of attacks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Emanuel’s Tel Aviv remarks as “deeply flawed,” stressing that they mostly recycle the idea that “Netanyahu is bad” and add little to the U.S. debate, while right-leaning outlets turn the same speech into a high-stakes warning, using charged phrases like “poisoning truth,” “lies,” “territorial pariah,” and “stay out” to portray him as lecturing Israelis and intruding on sovereignty.

The sharpest dividing lines are Emanuel’s claims that Israel faces a “dead end” or “cannot survive,” plus hints of broader policy and partisan motives; the right amplifies those as existential, the left de-emphasizes them.

Both sides agree the speech is politically significant, but they diverge over whether it is a critique or a threat, reflecting broader left-right splits over Netanyahu, Israel’s future, and U.S.-Israel alignment.
summary: On CBS's "Face The Nation," former U.S. Ambassador to Japan Rahm Emanuel outlined a new Middle East strategy, criticizing Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu while urging a "23-state solution" involving the Arab League.

Arguing Israel has "lost Europe," Emanuel labeled the nation a "pariah state," yet Israel's stock exchange is setting all-time highs this year, fueled by foreign investment and continued regional diplomatic breakthroughs.

Warning that West Bank construction leads to "perpetual conflict," Emanuel threatened sanctions against companies facilitating those communities, arguing Israel "cannot fight indefinitely" against global sentiment.
Contradicting Emanuel's claim that Israel is "less safe today, not more," critics point to Hezbollah's eradication and the deaths of Sinwar and Nasrallah as evidence of improved security.

Israel's claim to Judea and Samaria stems from the 1917 Balfour Declaration and 1922 League of Nations mandate, while the Abraham Accords with Gulf states provide a broader framework for regional peace.
title: Rahm Emanuel Says Israel Cannot Survive as a "Territorial Pariah," Calls for New Path Forward

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Abu Safiya as a humanitarian victim of deliberate, possibly lethal mistreatment, using charged terms like “imminent danger,” “deliberate,” and “scandalous,” and often broadening the story into attacks on doctors, hospitals, and calls for sanctions.
Right-Leaning coverage pivots toward security suspicion, with phrases like “Hamas colonel” and hints of “systematic targeting” or militant imagery, treating the case through a counterterror lens.
Center coverage stays more restrained, stressing “arbitrary” detention and UN/legal findings.
All sides agree he is detained and that UN bodies want his release, but ideology divides the interpretation of why.
summary: On Wednesday, a United Nations inquiry labeled the 18-month detention of Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, director of Gaza's Kamal Adwan Hospital, arbitrary and urged his immediate release, citing violations of international human rights instruments.

Israeli forces seized Abu Safiya in December 2024, alleging the pediatrician was a Hamas member, though rights groups note authorities have produced no public evidence supporting these combatant claims during his detention without charge.

Lawyer Nasser Odeh reported after a July 2 prison visit that the doctor is nearly unrecognizable, suffering severe injuries to his 'head, eyes, ears, and neck' while struggling to breathe in the Rakefet interrogation facility.
Multiple UK Members of Parliament, including Jeremy Corbyn, former Labour leader, and Nadia Whittome, Labour MP, have urged the government to use diplomatic leverage to secure Abu Safiya's release.

The UN inquiry warned this case 'may indicate a widespread or systematic practice' of arbitrary detention, while UN Special Rapporteur Francesca Albanese criticized the BBC for presenting unproven Israeli military claims regarding the doctor.
title: UN inquiry urges release of Gaza doctor held by Israel since December 2024

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Graham’s death as a broader political inflection point, stressing “power shift,” “what’s coming next,” and “ health transparency” to suggest institutional transition and accountability; right-leaning coverage pushes the same story into a sharper age critique, using phrases like “geriatric politicians” and “age issue” to underscore elite decline.
The center is more reportorial, adding concrete details like McConnell’s hospitalization, delayed disclosure, and the aortic tear.

Across all sides, the shared emphasis is Congress’s aging leadership, but the ideological divide is whether that signals a looming Washington reset, a transparency problem, or a blunt generational crisis.
summary: On Saturday, July 11, Senator Lindsey Graham , 71, died suddenly from an aortic tear, shocking colleagues in Congress where he had served since 2003.
Graham's unexpected passing reignited concerns about the aging Congress, the oldest in modern history, where nearly one-quarter of the 530 voting members are 70 or older.
Senate Minority Leader Mitch McConnell, 84, revealed he was recovering from a fall and pneumonia after weeks of speculation, prompting Senator John Cornyn to demand transparency.
Former Chicago mayor Rahm Emanuel renewed calls for mandatory retirement at 75 for federal officials, arguing legislators should "get out of Washington" rather than serve during decline.
Majority Democrats plans campaigns targeting older lawmakers, reflecting a push for generational change as voters remain anxious about economic security and the average senator's age is about 65.
title: After Lindsey Graham’s death, questions linger about aging politicians and health transparency

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Butler as a case study in conspiracy culture and institutional questions, using phrases like “bizarre rant,” “conspiracy theories,” and “new Dallas 1963” to spotlight Trump’s remarks and the Secret Service’s reform, while right-leaning coverage casts the same event as an ongoing threat-and-survival story, leaning on “assassination attempt,” “threats,” “they blew it,” and “God was with me” to stress peril, blame, and providence.

The sharpest dividing lines are whether the focus is on misinformation and accountability or on victimization and heroism, with the right also amplifying the Inspector General’s lapses and the left de-emphasizing specific failure details.
Both sides agree Butler remains a live security flashpoint, but they situate it within different ideological worlds: skepticism and systems versus persecution and resilience.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump launched into a bizarre rant about Thomas Crooks, describing the would-be assassin as a "very strange cookie" while praising him as a "great student" with "almost perfect" academic records.

Trump criticized the Secret Service for failing to secure the Butler, Pennsylvania, rally venue two years prior, after a Department of Homeland Security Office of Inspector General report revealed agents missed 102 radio transmissions about the suspect.

While condemning the planning failures, Trump praised the Secret Service agents who shielded him with their bodies, calling them "very brave," and credited the counter-sniper who eliminated the shooter within four seconds from 300 yards.

Renewed scrutiny followed recently obtained FBI records revealing Crooks exchanged two emails with a Butler County Sheriff deputy before the attempt, igniting backlash within MAGA circles and intensifying criticism from commentator Tucker Carlson.

The assassination attempt remains part of a broader pattern of threats; in September 2024, Secret Service agents averted another attack by would-be assassin Ryan Routh at Trump's Florida golf club, resulting in his life sentence plus 84 months.
title: Trump Marks Two Years Since Butler Assassination Attempt, Says 'God Was With Me'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s 59 percent claim as deception, using sharp phrases like “baselessly claims,” “Lying Trump,” and “exact opposite” to mock his approval boast and his price talk.
Right-Leaning coverage is cooler but still skeptical, stressing the gap between the “actual approval rating” and “what he says,” while de-emphasizing inflation and broader implications.
The center adds a more measured, concrete wrinkle with polling around 40 percent and a warning about midterm trouble.
All three agree the numbers clash with polling, but they diverge on whether the story is a lie, a mismatch, or a political risk.
summary: On Sunday, President Donald Trump claimed a 59 percent approval rating in a Truth Social post, despite no major polling averages supporting this figure.
The Times average as of Monday morning shows 39 percent approval and 58 percent disapproval; experts suggest Trump may have misinterpreted his disapproval rating as approval.

Tahra Hoops, a director at the Chamber of Progress, questioned the accuracy, asking if he "accidentally mix up the numbers," while Nate Cohn, The Times' chief political analyst, noted Trump's ratings have fallen to a historic floor.
title: Trump Makes New Claim About His Approval Rating

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump’s notification as a “new war” and a constitutional showdown, stressing “war powers,” “without Congress,” and “resumption of hostilities,” while right-leaning coverage is more hawkish and expansive, using phrases like “back on,” “escalated again,” “military pressure,” and “Iranian blockade” to situate the move within regional brinkmanship and sanction politics .
The center is comparatively procedural, emphasizing the formal restart and the 60-day clock.
Both sides agree on renewed action and Congress’s role, but diverge sharply over whether the story is executive overreach or strategic escalation in a broader Middle East conflict.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump formally notified Congress that military action against Iran restarted on July 7, following his declaration that the monthslong ceasefire is "over."

Trump claimed Iran violated their agreement by attacking three oil tankers last week; the administration previously argued hostilities had "terminated," thereby negating the 60-day War Powers Resolution deadline.
The U.S. will reimpose a targeted "Iranian blockade" in the Strait of Hormuz and impose a 20% fee on commercial shipments, after American forces struck more than 300 Iranian targets in the last week.

Regional tensions intensified after Houthi forces attacked Abha International Airport in Saudi Arabia in retaliation for airstrikes on Sanaa International Airport, while warning of "air sanctions" against Saudi airports.

Democrats are considering legal options to force compliance, as Congress previously passed a resolution seeking to limit military action; the administration describes the strikes as "limited, measured, planned, and executed.
title: Trump formally informs Congress of Iran war resumption

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the shooting as a troubling pattern of “deadly force,” stressing terms like “killed,” “fatal,” and even body-camera skepticism—“lost the benefit of the doubt”—while humanizing the victim as a “26-year-old Colombian man” and noting a possible child nearby.

Right-Leaning coverage is narrower and more corrective, centering the claim that he was “not the target” and contrasting it with an “earlier DHS account,” using a cooler tone but still signaling institutional controversy.

The sharpest divide is whether the event is portrayed as ICE overreach or a disputed enforcement mistake; both sides agree on the fatal shooting and warrant issue, reflecting broader trust-versus-order ideological tensions.
summary: On Monday, July 13, 2026, a federal immigration officer fatally shot a motorist in Biddeford, Maine, marking the second such incident in a week involving U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents.
Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin told Sen. Angus King that the officer opened fire after the man attempted to use his vehicle as a weapon while being pursued for deportation.
Witnessing from a third-floor window, Daniel Boucher reported hearing "pop, pop, pop" and saw a small car "turned 90 degrees to the curb" with an SUV behind it.

Monday's killing marks the ninth death from an encounter with federal immigration officials since President Donald Trump returned to office 18 months ago, including the July 7 fatal shooting of Salgado Araujo.
ICE arrested 546 people in Maine between the start of Trump's second term and March 11, 2026, with about 45% of those arrested having criminal backgrounds, according to analyzed data.
title: Man shot and killed by ICE in Maine was not the target of warrant, Sen. Angus King says

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the announcement as “cryptically” or “mystery”-laden, leaning into suspense and speculation about hidden motives, including possible election-interference revelations or war signaling; right-leaning coverage instead treats it as a serious, dignified “primetime speech” and even teases an “extremely important” address, often pairing it with escalation language like “major flare-up” and blockade details.
Center coverage sits between them, emphasizing the Iran strategy context and “next steps” with less drama.
The sharpest dividing lines are the undisclosed topic, Iran escalation, and whether the speech is a national security moment or a political teaser.
All sides agree it is a Thursday primetime address amid rising U.S.-Iran tension.
summary: President Donald Trump announced via Truth Social on Monday that he will deliver a Speech to the Nation on Thursday at 9 P.M. Eastern, though he provided no specific reason for the prime-time address.

Trump recently declared the United States the 'Guardian of the Hormuz Strait,' establishing a policy to keep the Strait open 'with or without Iran' while requiring other nations to reimburse 20% of cargo value for security.

This policy follows United States military strikes on Iran over the weekend, which officials cited as necessary to protect commercial shipping through the strategic waterway. Escalations in the war in Iran have dominated recent headlines.

The upcoming address recalls President Trump's remarks from the White House Cross Hall in April regarding the Iran war, marking another high-profile update on the conflict during intense military activity.

While the White House has not confirmed the subject of the remarks, the ongoing war in Iran and shifting maritime security policies remain central to the current agenda as observers await further details.
title: Trump says he will give primetime speech Thursday amid flare-up with Iran

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Hyperion as a costly corporate gamble, stressing “generous tax incentives,” “splits a town,” and housing pressure that can “price others out,” while right-leaning coverage recasts the same expansion as a win, leaning on “boosting rural community,” scholarships, and job counts, including more than 1,000 permanent jobs.
The left highlights social disruption and public subsidy concerns; the right foregrounds local uplift and Meta ’s goodwill messaging.
Center coverage sits between them, emphasizing scale, 7,500 construction jobs, and Meta covering energy costs.
Both sides agree on the $50 billion, 5-gigawatt buildout, but diverge sharply on whether it signals overreach or opportunity.
summary: On Monday, Meta announced it will expand its Hyperion data center in Richland Parish, Louisiana, to a 5GW facility costing over $50 billion, making it the company's largest data center project.
Previously, the project was planned as a 2GW facility costing $27 billion through a joint venture with Blue Owl Capital Inc. to manage the buildout and facility operations.

Since construction began in December 2024, local businesses in Louisiana have received over $1.6 billion in contracts from Meta. The company pledged over $1 billion for infrastructure improvements including roads and water systems.
Bloomberg reported Meta is developing plans for a cloud infrastructure business , potentially selling access to 'raw' computing capacity to outsiders akin to neocloud competitors like CoreWeave Inc.
Chief Executive Officer Mark Zuckerberg has pledged to spend at least $600 billion on US infrastructure projects over the next few years to achieve his goal of AI superintelligence.
title: Meta expands Louisiana data center to 5 gigawatts, investment crosses $50 billion

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the task force as an aggressive crackdown on whistleblowers and press freedom, using loaded terms like “infamous leaker,” “hunts journalists,” and “full force of law” to cast Hegseth as punitive and anti-media, even adding subpoena details to heighten the escalation.
Right-Leaning coverage treats the same move as a justified law-and-order response, stressing “track down,” “prosecute,” and “national security,” with claims that leaks endanger troops and operations.
The sharpest divide is whether leaks signal accountability or betrayal.
Both sides agree a joint DOJ- Pentagon task force targets media leaks, but they split over whether it protects the public or suppresses dissent.
summary: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth announced a joint task force with the Justice Department to identify and prosecute government information leakers.
Hegseth assigned the Defense Department's general counsel to lead internal leak investigations, and departments have 48 hours to respond to information requests.
The announcement follows subpoenas issued to New York Times reporters over their reporting on security issues regarding President Trump's new Air Force One plane.
Hegseth stated that access to confidential information is a sacred trust and that those who betray it will face the full force of the law.
title: Hegseth announces joint task force to identify and prosecute leakers
